result:
Good — confirms `atomicGet`/`atomicSet` compile to C11 `_Atomic`/GCC `__atomic`/`__sync` builtins (lock-free atomic ops), not mutex-based. Now I have everything needed.

## Shared state between main thread and bio background threads (bio.c)

**Job queue state (per-worker), guarded by `pthread_mutex_t bio_mutex[worker]` + `pthread_cond_t bio_newjob_cond[worker]`:**
- `bio_jobs[worker]` (list*) — main thread pushes via `bioSubmitJob`/`listAddNodeTail`; bg thread pops via `listFirst`/`listDelNode`. Lock held for all access.
- `bio_jobs_counter[type]` (unsigned long array) — incremented by main (`bioSubmitJob`), decremented by bg thread after processing; read by main in `bioPendingJobsOfType`. Same mutex.
- `bio_newjob_cond[worker]` — condvar main thread signals on submit, bg thread signals after removing a job (also used by `bioDrainWorker` on main thread to wait for empty queue). Paired with the same mutex.

**Completion-queue state, guarded by `pthread_mutex_t bio_mutex_comp`:**
- `bio_comp_list` (list*) — bg thread appends `bio_comp_item` on completion jobs; main thread (event-loop callback `bioPipeReadJobCompList`) swaps it out for a fresh list under the same lock.

**Wake-up pipe, no mutex (kernel-buffer-synchronized, fixed after creation):**
- `job_comp_pipe[2]` — created once in `bioInit` (main thread) before any bg thread exists; bg thread only writes to `job_comp_pipe[1]`, main thread (event loop) only reads `job_comp_pipe[0]`. Correctness relies on the pipe being set up before `pthread_create`, and best-effort semantics (write failure on full pipe is intentionally ignored) — no lock needed since each side only touches its own fd end.

**Thread handles, effectively no synchronization:**
- `bio_threads[BIO_WORKER_NUM]` — written once by main thread in `bioInit` right after each `pthread_create`; read later by `bioKillThreads` (called from crash/signal path) doing `pthread_cancel`/`pthread_join`. No lock — relies on happens-before from thread creation and single-writer-before-use ordering.

**Atomically-shared fields in the global `server` struct (declared `redisAtomic`), synchronized via C11 `_Atomic`/GCC atomic builtins, not mutexes:**
- `server.aof_bio_fsync_status` — set by bg thread (`BIO_AOF_FSYNC`/`BIO_CLOSE_AOF` handling), read/reset by main thread (aof.c, server.c) — `atomicGet`/`atomicSet`.
- `server.aof_bio_fsync_errno` — set by bg thread on fsync failure, read by main thread — `atomicGet`/`atomicSet`.
- `server.fsynced_reploff_pending` — set by bg thread on successful fsync, also set directly by main thread in several places (aof.c, server.c) — `atomicGet`/`atomicSet`. Comments in aof.c/config.c explicitly flag this as a documented race between main-thread and bio-worker updates that's tolerated by design (only the "largest" pending offset matters).

**Read-only after startup — no synchronization needed:**
- `server.bio_cpulist` — set once from config before `bioInit()` spawns threads (immutable config, can't change at runtime); read by bg thread once via `redisSetCpuAffinity`.
- `bio_worker_title[]`, `bio_job_to_worker[]` — `static` const-after-init arrays, read by both sides but never written after program start.
- Per-job `bio_job` structs themselves are not shared: each is heap-allocated (`zmalloc`) by the main thread, handed off via the mutex-protected list, and freed (`zfree`) solely by the bg thread — ownership transfers, no concurrent access to the same job struct.

Note: `errno` used in both threads is thread-local per POSIX/glibc, so despite looking global it isn't actually shared state.
